course_description: Introduces professional accounting & management students to both microeconomics & macroeconomics; highlights practical applicability of economics to analysis of contemporary problems; market trends & government policies.

requirements.assumed_background: No previous study in the field of economics, micro or macro, is required.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information Lecture
Day: Thursday 10 am - 12 noon (27 July 2012 - 25 Oct 2012)

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The primary aim of this course is to provide students with a clear understanding of the basic micro and macroeconomic theories and of how theories relate to real world economic activities. The market mechanism and how it operates to efficienctly allocate scarce resources provides the theoretical foundation to the course with market failure shown to explain real world economic problems. At the end of the course, students should be able to apply these theoretical tools into the real world context to understand, from an economics perspective, the role of markets and of government in a regulatory role.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a clear understanding of the fundamentals of economic theories of consumption, production, resource and distribution markets; investment, inflation and demand and supply; and relate them to real world business and regulatory activities.
Apply the tools of economic analysis to critically evaluate the current consumption decisions of individuals, and the factor employment, output, cost, price and profit decisions of businesses.
Develop appropriate strategies for entrepreneurs and enterprise managers and make recommendations for policy changes in all areas to maximise returns from business activities.
Understand and explain the operation of the enterprises and the changes taking place in the international economic, social and business environments.
Apply general economic concepts and analysis to business operations to enable you to work as a competent financial manager or executive of a company.
Consider investment, output, price and profit strategies of a firm or corporation to cope with the effects of internal and external changes that can impact on a company's performance.
Understand and appreciate the role of regulatory agencies within the Australian economy e.g. the RBA and ACCC; and the global economy e.g. WTO.

assessment_summary.title: Tutorial presentation

assessment_details.title: Multiple-Choice and short answers
assessment_details.task_description:
A mid-semester exam including MCQs and short answer questions will be held to provide early feedback to students on their progress.
Venue and time: In lecture room, during normal lecture time

Duration of exam: 80 min (including 10 minutes persusal)

Examinable material: Lecture and tutorial material Lectures 1-6 Ch 1-6 Bajada et al.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Dates for presentations to be allocated in Tutorial 1
assessment_details.task_description:
Task Description: Oral presentation by each student of solutions to pre-assigned tutorial questions for one week and written submission of the same materials.

In Tutorial 1 (Week 2), students will be organised into groups. Each group will be allocated one week's tutorial topic. Each student in the group will be assigned a question.

Each presenter must submit to the tutor written copies of answers which are presented orally during the tutorial.

Marks are awarded on the basis of the quality of the verbal presentation and written answers.

Criteria & Marking: Criteria: (a) clarity of argument; (b) step by step approach in presentation of answer and in writing; (c) use of formula and diagram(s) as and when necessary; (d) appropriate writing style to adequately convey the answer.

Marking: 7 marks for written paper; 3 marks for presentation.

assessment_details.title: Markets in action
assessment_details.task_description:
Task Description:

Write a 1,000 word essay using the tools of economic analysis to describe, and relevant diagrams to illustrate, the changes in the product market that could result from advertising .

In your essay refer to ALL of the following points:

* The factors that influence the price elasticity of demand.

* Why and how advertisers try to change the consumer demand of a product, and how could they change the consumer demand curve?

* Describe two real-life examples of advertisments and what do you think the strategies are meant to achieve in each case?

* Overall, what are the costs and benefits of such market strategies to (a) consumers and (b) the company supplying the product?

Criteria & Marking:

(a) clarity of argument; (b) use of diagram(s) as and when necessary; (c) appropriate writing

style to adequately convey the answer; (d) evidence of reading outside the text book; (e) correct and complete referencing of all sources.

Marking: Total marks - 15 marks

learning_resources.required_resources: Bajada, Chris; Jackson, J; McIver, R and Wilson E. 2012. Economic Principles 3rd edition, McGraw-Hill, Australia.

Essentials of Economics / Hubbard, Garnett, Lewis, O'Brien. Pearson Australia, 2010

Principles of Economics / J. Jackson, R. McIver, C. Bajada. 2nd edn (Call no. HC605 .E26 2007)

Principles of Economics / N. Gregory Mankiw (Call No. HB171.5 .M264 2004)

Economics / Douglas McTaggart, Christopher Findlay, Michael Parkin (Call No. HB172 .M398 2007)

Understanding Economics / Russell S. Sobel et al. (Call No. HB171.5 .U53 2006)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
There is an increasing reliance on micro and macroeconomic theories in government, business and personal decision-making to improve resource allocation decisions. Equally, when there is evidence of misallocation of resources, there is reliance on economic theories to explain how and why the problem has emerged. The aim of this course is to introduce students to relevant micro and macroeconomic theories and their applications. The assessment is designed to test the student's understanding of economic concepts and ability to apply these concepts to real world situations.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The online quiz assessment is replaced by one mid-semester exam comprising multiple-choice questions and short answers; this exam is weighted at 20%. The final exam is weighted 55%.

other_learning_activities_information:
This course will be taught using the following modes:

(1) Lectures

The lectures will provide students with a clear understanding of the relevant micro and macroeconomic theories and their links to the decision making process in real world business, trade and commerce.

(2) Tutorials

In tutorials, students apply their theoretical knowledge to solve problems dealing with real world economic issues. Students should consult the course website on Blackboard for the tutorial questions.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Range of marks: 0% - 19.99%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Range of marks: 20% - 44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Range of marks: 45% - 49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Range of marks: 50% - 64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Range of marks: 65% - 74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Range of marks: 75% - 84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Range of marks: 85% - 100%
late_submission:
Essays submitted after the due date will be penalised for each day late unless prior agreement for late submission has been received from the course coordinator.

Late penalty: minus 1 mark for 1 day, minus 2 marks for 2 days etc. including weekends.

** Computer failure and no back-ups are not acceptable reasons for lateness**
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

The tutorial work, summary and the essay MUST ALL be completed for a grade greater than 4 to be achieved.

SUBMISSION OF ASSIGNMENTS

Unless otherwise specified, all assignments/essays must be submitted electronically via Blackboard. The instructions for submission are in the Online Submission Folder in your course Blackboard site. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this course profile.

For this course, students are required to submit a hard copy to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre in addition to the electronic copy.

All assignments must be submitted by the due date and time stated in the course profile.

1. Each assignment submitted to FCLC must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed Economics Assignment Cover Sheet, available online from www.bel.uq.edu.au or at the FCLC.
2. All assignments will be stamped with the date of receipt, or if submitted after hours with the date of the NEXT business day.
3. Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work.

4. Incomplete or incorrect assignment coversheets can cause delays in processing your assignments.

5. Students must produce their student ID card (or driver's licence) to collect marked assignments.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time. Note that the mid-semester exam is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM.

Information about deferred examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 and on the School of Economics website at http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/policy-on-absences-from-mid-semester-examinations Please read both these websites very carefully.

All applications for deferred exams must be submitted within (5) calendar days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-Semester Examination (SCHOOL exam ) - submit your application to your course coordinator with appropriate documentation attached. Do not submit applications to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam ) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with the appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (12-14 December).
